summary: Gruber dissects Tim Cook's opening statement at the 2020 Congressional antitrust hearing, finding it partly sincere and partly strategic positioning. He praises Cook's 'best not most' framing but argues Apple's App Store policies — particularly the rule forbidding apps from even explaining how users can sign up on the web — undermine Cook's claim that Apple only builds things it's proud of. Gruber calls out Cook's omission of internet software distribution as 'flat-out dishonest,' noting that Cook framed the App Store as replacing brick-and-mortar retail while ignoring the thriving direct web sales model. He also questions Cook's citation of Google and Huawei as smartphone competitors and his implicit argument that lacking monopoly market share means antitrust scrutiny is unwarranted.
key_points: Cook's 'making the best not the most' line is true but conveniently doubles as an antitrust defense since Apple lacks majority market share in any category
Apple's 'reader app' rules are indefensible — apps cannot even tell users in plain text how to sign up on the web, forcing absurd workarounds like phone support lines
Cook's framing of the App Store as replacing brick-and-mortar distribution dishonestly omits the thriving direct web sales model that predated it by over a decade
The 'quality department store' analogy for the App Store is laughable given the store's actual content ranges from premium to junk
Cook's implicit argument — no monopoly means no antitrust problem — ignores that Apple has achieved dominant profits with minority market share
money_quotes: It is prima facie wrong that one of the rules is that an app is not allowed to explain the rules.
Is Apple proud of this rule against explaining the rules, and do they enjoy explaining it to their friends and family?
Apple should be earning its share of in-app subscription revenue by competing on convenience, not confusion and obfuscation.
No M-word, but the meaning is clear: Apple has no monopoly so why are they here?
The analogy to a 'quality department store' holds as much water as a sieve.

Talking about brick-and-mortar software distribution without even mentioning direct downloads and sales over the web is flat-out dishonest, and clearly the most disappointing aspect of Cook's prepared testimony.
They don't sell the most things but they do make the most money, so it's not like 'most' doesn't apply to Apple.
Talking about brick-and-mortar software sales in 2020 is like talking about when cars sucked because you had to crank their motors by hand to start them.
key_insight: Cook's testimony is strategically crafted to position Apple as a minority-share player undeserving of antitrust scrutiny, but his omission of web distribution and defense of rules that forbid apps from explaining the rules reveal the gap between Apple's stated values and its App Store practices.
